section.title: The Discourse on the Church
section.sectionRationale: The fourth great discourse turns from Jesus' identity to the life of His community. Greatness is childlike humility, little ones must never be despised or made to stumble, the straying are sought, sin between brothers is addressed with patient discipline, and forgiveness among the forgiven has no limit.
section.thesis: In the community of the King, greatness is humble childlikeness, the least are precious and pursued, sin is confronted for the sake of restoration, and those forgiven an unpayable debt must forgive their brothers from the heart.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the greatest is the humble child; the gravity of causing little ones to stumble; the Father's pursuit of the straying sheep; confronting a sinning brother and the authority of the church; and the parable of the unforgiving servant.

verses.text: At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who then is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?”
Jesus invited a little child to stand among them.
“Truly I tell you,” He said, “unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven.
Therefore, whoever {{tapeinoo|humbles}} himself like this little child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven.
And whoever welcomes a little child like this in My name welcomes Me.

units.label: Causes of sin
units.note: woe to those who make little ones stumble

verses.text: But if anyone causes one of these little ones who believe in Me to stumble, it would be better for him to have a large millstone hung around his neck and to be drowned in the depths of the sea.
Woe to the world for the causes of sin. These stumbling blocks must come, but woe to the man through whom they come!
If your hand or your foot causes you to sin, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life crippled or lame than to have two hands and two feet and be thrown into the eternal fire.
And if your eye causes you to sin, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.
See that you do not look down on any of these little ones. For I tell you that their angels in heaven always see the face of My Father in heaven.
What do you think? If a man has a hundred sheep and one of them goes astray, will he not leave the ninety-nine on the hills and go out to search for the one that is lost?
And if he finds it, truly I tell you, he rejoices more over that one sheep than over the ninety-nine that did not go astray.
In the same way, your Father in heaven is not willing that any of these little ones should perish.

units.label: A brother who sins
units.note: confront, restore, and the authority of the church

verses.text: If your brother sins against you, go and confront him privately. If he listens to you, you have won your brother over.
But if he will not listen, take one or two others along, so that ‘every matter may be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.’
If he refuses to listen to them, tell it to the {{ekklesia|church}}. And if he refuses to listen even to the church, regard him as you would a pagan or a tax collector.
Truly I tell you, whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven, and whatever you loose on earth will be loosed in heaven.
Again, I tell you truly that if two of you on the earth agree about anything you ask for, it will be done for you by My Father in heaven.
For where two or three gather together in My name, there am I with them.”
Then Peter came to Jesus and asked, “Lord, how many times shall I forgive my brother who sins against me? Up to seven times?”
Jesus answered, “I tell you, not just seven times, but seventy-seven times!
Because of this, the kingdom of heaven is like a king who wanted to settle accounts with his servants.
As he began the settlements, a debtor owing ten thousand talents was brought to him.
Since the man was unable to pay, the master ordered that he be sold to pay his debt, along with his wife and children and everything he owned.
Then the servant fell on his knees before him. ‘Have patience with me,’ he begged, ‘and I will pay back everything.’
His master had compassion on him, {{aphiemi|forgave}} his debt, and released him.
But when that servant went out, he found one of his fellow servants who owed him a hundred denarii. He grabbed him and began to choke him, saying, ‘Pay back what you owe me!’
So his fellow servant fell down and begged him, ‘Have patience with me, and I will pay you back.’
But he refused. Instead, he went and had the man thrown into prison until he could pay his debt.
When his fellow servants saw what had happened, they were greatly distressed, and they went and recounted all of this to their master.
Then the master summoned him and said, ‘You wicked servant! I forgave all your debt because you begged me.
Shouldn’t you have had mercy on your fellow servant, just as I had on you?’
In anger his master turned him over to the jailers to be tortured, until he should repay all that he owed.
That is how My heavenly Father will treat each of you unless you forgive your brother from your heart.”

tapeinoo.gloss: To make low, to humble. Greatness in the kingdom is inverted: it belongs to the one who lowers himself like a small child, dependent and unassuming, rather than to the one who grasps for status.

ekklesia.gloss: The assembly, the church, the gathered community of Jesus' people. Here it is the body before which unrepented sin is finally brought, entrusted with the authority to bind and loose, that is, to affirm or withhold fellowship in Christ's name.

aphiemi.gloss: To send away, release, cancel, forgive. It is used both for the king cancelling the vast debt and for the forgiveness God's people owe one another; those released from an unpayable debt must release their brothers.

basic.context: The disciples ask who is greatest. Jesus sets a child among them and says greatness is childlike humility. He warns solemnly against causing little ones to stumble and against sin in ourselves. He tells of a shepherd who leaves ninety-nine to seek one stray, showing the Father's heart. He gives a process for confronting a sinning brother, and the church's authority to bind and loose. Then, answering Peter, He teaches limitless forgiveness through the parable of a servant forgiven a huge debt who refuses to forgive a small one.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the fourth of Matthew's five discourses, and it is about community life, not individual piety alone. Read the parts together: humility, protecting the vulnerable, seeking the stray, restoring the sinner, and forgiving without limit all flow from grace received. The parable's ending is a sober warning, not a denial of grace: those truly forgiven will forgive.
basic.originalLanguages: Greatness belongs to the one who tapeinoo, humbles himself like a child. Serious sin is brought at last before the ekklesia, the church, which binds and looses in Christ's name. And the whole chapter turns on aphiemi, to release or forgive, the vast debt cancelled by the king and the debts we owe each other.
basic.typology: The seeking shepherd fulfills God's promise in Ezekiel 34 to seek His scattered sheep Himself. The unpayable debt cancelled pictures the forgiveness of sins that the cross secures.
basic.christ: Jesus is the humble one who became a child and a servant, the Good Shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to seek the one, and He is present wherever two or three gather in His name. The king's mercy in the parable is the mercy Jesus purchases.
basic.god: The Father does not will that one little one perish and rejoices over the found. He is the King who cancels an unpayable debt out of compassion, and He requires that the forgiven forgive, for a heart that refuses mercy has not truly received it.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 34 promises God will seek His straying sheep. Deuteronomy 19 gives the two or three witnesses. Colossians 3 and Ephesians 4 call the forgiven to forgive as God forgave them. Luke 15 tells the lost sheep as joy over one repentant sinner.
basic.oneStory: Having revealed who He is, Jesus shapes the community He is building. Its greatness is humility, its care reaches the least and the straying, its discipline aims at restoration, and its air is forgiveness, because everyone in it has been forgiven a debt they could never pay. This is the family the crucified and risen King gathers, and it lives by the mercy it has received.

context.literary: The fourth discourse, marked off by Matthew's usual closing formula in the next chapter, gathers Jesus' teaching on community. It follows the passion predictions and the disciples' jockeying for status, which the opening question about greatness exposes. The unit moves from humility to the protection, pursuit, restoration, and forgiveness of one another, and it climaxes in a parable that grounds all of it in grace received.
context.historical: A millstone turned by a donkey was a huge stone; drowning with it meant certain death, and drowning was regarded with horror. The two or three witnesses reflect the legal standard of Deuteronomy 19:15. Ten thousand talents was an astronomical, unpayable sum, perhaps billions in today's terms, against a hundred denarii, a few months' wages, dramatizing the disproportion between what we are forgiven and what we withhold.
context.canonical: The seeking shepherd fulfills Ezekiel 34, where God pledges to search for His scattered sheep. The witnesses procedure applies Deuteronomy 19:15. The binding and loosing echoes Matthew 16:19. The call to forgive as one has been forgiven runs through Ephesians 4:32 and Colossians 3:13, and the parable illustrates the petition of the Lord's Prayer, forgive us as we forgive.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To form the life of Jesus' community around humility, the protection and pursuit of the vulnerable, loving discipline aimed at restoration, and wholehearted forgiveness, all flowing from the immeasurable mercy God has shown in Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The discourse is strongly prescriptive: become like a child, do not cause stumbling, seek the stray, follow the steps of confrontation, forgive without counting. The parable both describes God's astonishing mercy and prescribes the forgiveness it demands.

debates.question: Does the unforgiving servant teach that a truly forgiven person can lose God's forgiveness?

views.label: A sober warning that reveals the heart
views.case: A persistent, hard refusal to forgive shows that a person never truly grasped or received God's mercy; the parable exposes a false, unregenerate heart rather than a genuinely forgiven one being unforgiven, consistent with justification by grace.

views.label: A real and serious threat of judgment
views.case: Jesus says plainly this is how the Father will treat each of you unless you forgive from the heart, and the church must feel the weight of that warning; unforgiveness is not a small thing but places one under judgment.

debates.bearing: Both agree that the forgiven must forgive and that unforgiveness is spiritually deadly; the passage functions as a searching warning that drives the professing believer back to the mercy of God, not a license to presume upon grace.

study.originalLanguages: The disciples want to know who is greatest, and Jesus answers by placing a child among them: the great one is the one who tapeinoo, lowers himself, becoming small and dependent. He guards these little ones fiercely, warns against every cause of stumbling, and reveals the Father who seeks the single stray. When sin persists, the matter comes at last before the ekklesia, the church, which binds and looses in His name and meets in the promise that He is present among them. And the whole is crowned by aphiemi, release and forgiveness: a king cancels a debt of ten thousand talents out of compassion, and the servant who will not cancel a hundred denarii is condemned. Those forgiven an infinite debt must forgive from the heart.

typology.type: The shepherd seeking the one stray
typology.fulfillment: God's own promise to search for His scattered sheep, fulfilled in Jesus the Good Shepherd, Ezekiel 34:11-16, Luke 15:4-7.
The forgiveness of sins secured at the cross, an infinite debt paid by Christ, Colossians 2:13-14.
Jesus Himself, who humbled Himself as a servant, the pattern of true greatness, Philippians 2:5-8.

study.christ: Jesus embodies everything He teaches here. He is the one who humbled Himself, becoming small and lowly, the very greatness He commends. He is the Good Shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to seek the one that has strayed and rejoices to carry it home. He is present wherever two or three gather in His name, the living center of His church, and He entrusts to that community His own authority to bind and loose. Above all He is the ground of the parable: the compassion of the king who cancels the unpayable debt is the mercy Jesus secures by His cross, so that a people forgiven everything might forgive one another from the heart.
study.god: The God of this discourse is a Father who treasures the least and the straying, unwilling that a single little one should perish, and rejoicing over the one that is found. He is the King whose compassion cancels a debt no servant could ever repay, and precisely because His mercy is so vast, He will not tolerate its recipients hoarding it. He requires forgiveness not as a cold condition but as the natural fruit of grace received; a heart that clutches its small grievances after being forgiven everything has not understood the mercy it claims.

crossReferences.note: God pledges to seek His scattered sheep, fulfilled in the seeking shepherd of the parable.
Every matter established by two or three witnesses, the basis for the confrontation steps.
Forgive one another as God in Christ forgave you, the heart of the unforgiving servant parable.
If you forgive others, your Father will forgive you, the petition the parable dramatizes.

study.oneStory: Having settled who Jesus is, the Gospel now shapes the community He gathers. It is a family whose greatness is humility, whose care reaches the least and the lost, whose discipline seeks restoration, and whose atmosphere is forgiveness, because every member has been forgiven a debt beyond paying. The seeking Father and the compassionate King are revealed in Jesus, who will cancel the debt at the cross, so that His people, released from an infinite debt, release one another from the heart.

quiz.q: How does Jesus define greatness in the kingdom?

options.text: Humbling oneself like a little child, low and dependent.
options.note: Verses 3-4 make childlike humility the mark of greatness.

options.text: Serving others in visible acts of ministry.
options.note: Service flows from it, but Jesus points to humility itself, becoming like a child, as the root.

options.text: Gaining influence and authority over others.
options.note: That is the disciples' assumption, which Jesus overturns.

options.text: Achieving the most in religious accomplishment.
options.note: Jesus commends lowliness, not accomplishment, as the path to greatness.

quiz.q: What does the parable of the lost sheep reveal about the Father?

options.text: He seeks the straying and is not willing that even one little one should perish.
options.note: Verses 12-14 show God's pursuing, rejoicing heart.

options.text: He cares about the whole flock and keeps them safe.
options.note: True, but the point is His pursuit of the single stray, which this general statement misses.

options.text: He is content to keep the ninety-nine and let the one go.
options.note: The opposite; He leaves the ninety-nine to search for the one.

options.text: He judges the stray for wandering away.
options.note: He seeks and rejoices over the stray rather than judging it.

quiz.q: What is the goal of confronting a sinning brother?

options.text: To win him back through patient, escalating steps, restoration not rejection.
options.note: Verses 15-17 aim first at winning the brother, involving the church only if he refuses.

options.text: To gather enough witnesses to prove he is guilty.
options.note: Witnesses are involved, but they serve restoration, not merely proving guilt.

options.text: To remove troublesome people from the church quickly.
options.note: The process is deliberately patient and aims at repentance, not swift removal.

options.text: To publicly shame the offender before everyone.
options.note: It begins privately and seeks to win the brother, not to shame him.

quiz.q: What is the main point of the parable of the unforgiving servant?

options.text: Those forgiven an unpayable debt by God must forgive their brothers from the heart.
options.note: Verses 32-35 tie our forgiveness of others to the mercy we have received.

options.text: We should forgive others a set number of times, like seventy-seven.
options.note: The number pictures limitless forgiveness, not a literal count, which this misreads.

options.text: God expects us to repay our debts to Him fully.
options.note: The debt is unpayable and cancelled by grace; the point is mercy, not repayment.

options.text: Forgiveness is optional for those who follow Jesus.
options.note: Jesus makes heartfelt forgiveness essential, not optional, for the forgiven.

quiz.q: What is promised where two or three gather in Jesus' name?

options.text: Jesus Himself is present among them.
options.note: Verse 20 promises His personal presence with the gathered community.

options.text: Their prayers will always get exactly what they ask.
options.note: There is a promise about agreed prayer, but the climax is His presence, and prayer is according to the Father's will.

options.text: They will have authority to do whatever they wish.
options.note: The authority to bind and loose serves Christ's purposes; it is not unlimited self-will.

options.text: A large crowd is needed for Jesus to be present.
options.note: Even two or three suffice; His presence is not a matter of numbers.

questions: Jesus set a child in the middle and called that greatness. Where is your desire for status quietly at odds with the humility He commends?
The Father seeks the one who strays. Is there someone drifting from Christ whom you have written off but God is still pursuing, perhaps through you?
Jesus gives careful steps for confronting sin, aimed at winning a brother. Where have you either avoided a needed conversation or handled one without love?
You have been forgiven a debt you could never repay. What grudge are you still holding that this mercy calls you to release?
Forgiveness here is from the heart, not just the lips. What would it look like to forgive someone deeply this week rather than merely tolerating them?

translationNotes.note: The BSB numbering goes from verse 10 to verse 12; verse 11, for the Son of Man came to save the lost, is not in the earliest manuscripts and appears as a footnote drawn from Luke 19:10.
The phrase may be rendered seventy-seven times or seventy times seven; either way it signifies limitless forgiveness, deliberately reversing Lamech's boast of unlimited vengeance in Genesis 4:24.
Ten thousand talents was an almost unimaginable sum, a talent being about twenty years' wages; the debt is designed to be unpayable, dwarfing the hundred denarii owed by the fellow servant.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 18:1-35. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; heading, line-break, and note markers were skipped. Verse 11 is omitted in the BSB (earliest manuscripts) and is carried in a translation note. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
